CalifOrnia
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
Septenber 13, 19 91
Linda Hedges
Planning Com:nissioner
City of Victorvil le
142L9 Deer Trail Drive
victorville, 
CA 92392
Rer Your Request for Advice
our Pile No. A-91-3 92
Dear Ms. Hedges:
This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
and confirms the telephone advice provided to you on August 13,
1991, regarding your duties and respons ibi L it ies under the
conf i ictlof - inieiest provisions of the Political 
Ref orrn Act (the
rra"grr . 1 I
OUESTIONS
1. Under the conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act, are
you required to disqualify yoursel'f fron participat ing 
- in
fovernriental decisi6ns regaiaing a donor to the nonprofit
ioundation of which you aie the volunteer presiilent of the board
of directors?
2. 
Do the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act require
you to disqualify yourself from participating in governmental
decisions iegarding issues you advocate publicly?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act do not
require you to disqualify yourself frorn participating in
goiernrneirtal decisions invofving a donor to the nonprofit entity
5f wtictr you are a volunteer president of the board of directors'
1t Government code Sections 81ooo-91015. AlI statutory references
are to the GovernEent code unless otherwise indicated' Corunission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et ses. A1I references to regulations are to Title 2,
Divi.sion 6 of the california code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322.5660


Our Fl■e No. A-9■-392
Page 2
2. The conf I ict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not
require you to disgualify yourself frorn participating in
governnental decisions regarding issues which you advocate
publicly.
FAC'TS
You are an appointed planning cornmissioner in the city of
VictorvilIe. 
You are a salaried enployee of a nonprofit
corporation situated in the city of Victorville 
which provides
treatment for nentally iIl adults.
In 1988 you founded another nonprofit corporation which is
unrelated to the corporation in which you are enployed. The
nonprofit corporation which you founded seeks to provide permanent
houling for the mentally iII homeless. This housing wiII.be
Iocated in a rural area outside the jurisdiction of the city of
victorville. 
You are currently president of the board of
directors of this nonprofit corporation. You receive no
compensation for your services to this nonprofit corporation.
A builder tho does business in the City of Victorville 
has
acted as an inforrnal consultant to the board of directors of the
nonprofit you created since its inception in 1988. This builder
is interesled in contributing cash or rnaterials to that nonprofit
corporation. You seek our advice to determine whether, in the
"ve-nt the builder nakes a donation to the nonprofit corporation,
you would be required to disqualify yourseJ'f from participating in
governroental decisions affecting the builder.
You also seek our advice to determine whether you are
required to disqualify yourself from participating in governrnental
deCisions regarding issues such as housing for the houeless due to
the fact thaf, you speak out publicly in support of such issues.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
from naking,
participating in, or using their officiat position to influence a
governnental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest. 
As a menber of the planninq
cornruission in the city of Victorville, 
you are a public official.
(Section 82048. )
Financial fnterests
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision vrithin the
neaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision wifl have a material financial effect, distinguishable


Our File No. A-91-392
Page 3
from its effect on the public generaLly, on the official, 
or on a
metnber of the official's 
innediate fa:nily,z or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the pubfic
official 
has a direct or indirect investrnent worth
one thousand dollars (51,000) or more.
(b) Any real Property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a counercial lendinq
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms avaitable to the public rrithout regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time r'rhen the
decision is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in vhich the public
official 
is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
enployee, or holds any position of nanagenent.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or.
agent'for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
tio hundred fifty dollars (5250) or lnore in value
provided to, rec-ived by, or prornised to the pubJ'ic
6tficiat 
within 12 Donths prior to the time shen
the decision is rnade.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investnent or interest Deans any investnent or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
putlic official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 1o-percent inlerest
or greater.
Section 87103.
Makinq. Particioatinq in Makincr, or Attemptinq to Influence a
Governrnental Decision
A public official 
Eakes a governnental decision or
participates in the naking of a governnental decision whenever the
2 An offi"ial's 
"imuediate fanilyrr are his or her spouse and
dependent ch j.ldren. 
(Section 82029.)


Our FiLe No. A-91-392
Page 4
public official 
votes on a Datter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreenent on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 187oo(b). Additionally, a public
official 
participates in a governnental decision e/hen, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or rnakes reconmendations to the
decision-naker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or naking any
investigation which requires the exercise of judg-
ment on the part of the official or designated
ernployee and the purpose of which is to influence
the decision,' or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official 
or designated eurployee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.
Regu).ation 18700 (c) .
With regard to a governrnental decision which is within or
before an ofiiciaL's 
agency or an agency apPointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official 
is
atternpting to uie his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherrrise attenpts to
influence any nenber, officer, enployee or consultant of the
agency. Att-mpts to influence incLude, but are not liEited to,
appeaiances or contacts by the official' on behalf of a business
entity, client, or custoner. (Regulation 18700.1.)
You are employed by a nonprofit corporation. For Purposes of
our analysis, we a-sune that you recej,ve income in excess of $250
in a l2-Donth period fron this nonprofit corporation.
Accordingly, ii it is reasonably foreseeable that the nonprofit
corporation which is a source of income to you will be affected
rnaterially by a decision, you must not only disqualify yourself
from participating in fornal decisions of the planning comrnission
which nay affect the nonprofit corporation, but you nust also
abstain fron attenpting to influence such decisions by
cornmunicating with other Denbers of the planning conmission or
city staff. regarding the decisions.
Foreseeabi l itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be


our File No. A-91-3 92
Page 5
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be Dore than a mere
possibility; 
however, certaintY is not required. 
(Downev Cares v.
Downev Communitv DeveloDment Cornm. (1987) 196 cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. llorrow (L977) 7o CaI.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
l FPPC Ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent Dore than actual
conflicts of interestr' it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conftict of interest. 
(witt v. I'Iorrow, suDra at 823-)
l,tater ia I itv
Regulation 18?02 sets forth the guidelines for deterrnining
whether an official's 
econonic interest in a decision is
rnaterially affected by a decision. If the official's 
econornic
interest is directlv invol.ved in the decision, Regulation r.a702':.
provides the appropiiate standard for assessing nateriality.
iowever, when the official's 
financial interests will be affected
indirectlv by a decision, the appropriate standards for
aeterrLning ruateriatity are those of Regulations 18702.2 through
:-a7o2.6- These regulations are discussed below.
Regulation ]-A702.1 provides that the effect of a decision is
material if a source of income to a public of,ficial of 5250 or
more in the preceding 12 months is directly involved in a decision
before the oificial's 
agency or there is a nexus between the
purpose for which the official 
receives income and the
|ovlrnmental decision. A source of income to a public official 
is
directly involved in a decision before an official's 
agency when
that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in uhich the
decision will be nade by filing an application,
cIain, appeal, or sinilar request or;
(2') Is a naEed party in, or is the -subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official's 
agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revoqation
of any license, peruit, or other entitlernent to, or
contract rrith, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702. 1(b) .
Accordingly, if the nonprofit corporation which is a source
of incorne to you appears before the planning connission, you are
required to disqualify yourself fron participating in such
decisions. You lrould also be required to disqualify yourself from
participating in decisions where there is a nexus bet$reen your


Our File No. A-91-3 9 2
Page 6
position rrith the nonprofit corporation and your official 
duties
as a planning commissioner. A nexus would exist, for example, if
the planning cornrnission had to make a decision regarding a project
you developed for the day treatnent center which is your employer.
Your disqual if ication would also be required if the nonprofit
corporation which is your empLoyer rrere affected indirectly by
decisions of the planning commission. This would be the case, for
example, if a decision pending before the planning commission
would indirectly affect the services provided by the nonprofit
corporation to nentally ill adults.
when a nonprofit entity is indirectly affected by a decision,
the standard foi deterruining nateriality is that of Regulation
La702.5. For exanple, if the nonprofit has gross annual receipts
of $1oo,oOO or 1ess,3 the effect of a decision will be material
It:
(1) The decision sill result in an increase
or decrease of the entity's gross annuaJ' receipts
for a fiscal year in the anount of $10,000 or more.
(2) The decision vrill cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional. expenses or to reduce or
elirninate existing expenses for a f j.scaI year in
the aroount of $2,500 or nore.
(3) The decision wilt result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities 
in the anount of S10,000 or nore.
Regulation 18702 .5 ( f) .
Therefore, if you deternine that a decision before the
planning comnission will indirectly affect the nonprofit
corporation which is a source of incone to you, your
disgual if ication is required if the decision uill affect the
nonfrofit corporation in the auounts indicated in Regulation
L87 02 .5 .
conversely, you are not reguired to disqualify yourself frou
participating in decisions affecting the nonprofit corporation for
which you volunteer your services and act as president of the
board of directors. 
Section 87103(d) provides that a public
official 
has an economic interest in any business entity in which
the officiaL is a director, officer, partner, trustee, enPloyee,
or holds any position of management. However, Section 82005
defines rrbusiness entityt' as an organization or enterprise
operated for profit. 
Since this nonprofit corporation is not a
3 of course, if
$100,000, other
the nonprofit's annual receipts r.rere greater than
provisions of Regulation 18702.5(f) would appIy.


Our File No. A-91-3 92
Page 7
business entity operated for profit, and since you receive no
incone fron this nonprofit corporation, you do not have a
disqualifying econornic interest in this nonprofit corporation.
Gifts
You have also asked whether your disqua I i f ication is required
when an individual or a business entity which has made a gift to
the nonprofit corporation for vhich you volunteer your services
appears before the planning conDission. As a general rule, donors
to the nonprofit corporation \.rould not be sources of j,ncome to
you. (Een Advice Letter, No. A-89-559. ) This is particularly
true in this instance, since you are not even compensated by the
nonprof it corPoration.
Public qenerallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is uaterial, disqual i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the pubJ.ic generally.
(section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all mernbers of
the public in the same nanner, disqua }i f ication nay be required
unless the effect of the decision on the public official's
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segEent of the public. 
(Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our
dilcussion, the public is the entire population of the city. 
(In
re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In re Or.ren (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Jorqensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017. )
Accordingly, in the case of the nonprofit corporation which
is a source oi incorue to you, your disqua I ification is required
only if the nonprofit corporation vill 
be materially affected in a
rnanner which is not siDilar to the effect of decisions of the
planning comnission on the general public.
Public Advocacv
As stated above, the Act only requires your disqua I i f ication
rrhen a governnental decision rrill have a naterial financial effect
on your economic j.nterests. Your advocacy of a Particular idea
does not constitute an econonic interest unless you receive income
for the purpose of advocating the particular issues. Accordingly,
without more, your advocacy of particular issues affecting the
honeless is not a disqualifying financial interest and you rnay
participate in decisions of the planning commission hlhich may have
an effect on such issues.


Our File No. A-91-3 92
Page 8
I.le trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901 .'l
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

appears before the planning conDission. As a general rule, donors
to the nonprofit corporation \.rould not be sources of j,ncome to
you. (Een Advice Letter, No. A-89-559. ) This is particularly
true in this instance, since you are not even compensated by the
nonprof it corPoration.
Public qenerallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is uaterial, disqual i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the pubJ.ic generally.
(section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all mernbers of
the public in the same nanner, disqua }i f ication nay be required
unless the effect of the decision on the public official's
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segEent of the public. 
(Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our
dilcussion, the public is the entire population of the city. 
(In
re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In re Or.ren (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Jorqensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017. )
Accordingly, in the case of the nonprofit corporation which
is a source oi incorue to you, your disqua I ification is required
only if the nonprofit corporation vill 
be materially affected in a
rnanner which is not siDilar to the effect of decisions of the
planning comnission on the general public.
Public Advocacv
As stated above, the Act only requires your disqua I i f ication
rrhen a governnental decision rrill have a naterial financial effect
on your economic j.nterests. Your advocacy of a Particular idea
does not constitute an econonic interest unless you receive income
for the purpose of advocating the particular issues. Accordingly,
without more, your advocacy of particular issues affecting the
honeless is not a disqualifying financial interest and you rnay
participate in decisions of the planning commission hlhich may have
an effect on such issues.


Our File No. A-91-3 92
I.le trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
